and the water is boiled, which fills the can with steam. The can is then sealed and allowed Vacuum to cool. Why does the can collapse as it cools? It is the atmospheric pressure that crum- ples the can: When the can is cooled after being sealed s0 that no air can flow in, the wa- ter vapor (steam) condenses t0 very small volume of liquid water: As gas, the waler filled the can but when it is condensed t0 liquid, the liquid does not come close to fill - ing the can The H,O molecules formerly present as gas are now collected in very h = 760 mm Hg small volume of liquid, and there are vety few molecules of gas left tO exert pressure out- for standard ward and counteract the air pressure As result; the pressure exerted by the gas mole- atmosphere cules in the atmosphere smashes the can_ device t0 measure atmospheric pressure the barometer; was invented in 1643 by an Italian scientist named Evangelista Torricelli ( 1608-1647). who had been student of Galileo Torricelli  s barometer is constructed by filling glass tube with liquid mercury and inverting it in dish of mercury as shown in Fig: 5.2. Notice that large quantity of mercury stays in the tube: In fact; at sea level the height of this column of mercury aver- ages 760 mm: Why does this mercury stay in the tube_ seemingly in defiance of gravity? Figure 5.2 illustrates how the pressure exerted by the atmospheric gases on the surface of mercury in the dish keeps the mercury in the tube: Atmospheric pressure results from the mass of the air being- pulled toward the center of the earth by gravity other words it results from the weight of the air: Changing FIGURE 5.2 A torricellian barometer: The tube; weather conditions cause the atmospheric pressure tO vary so the height of the column of com- pletely filled with mercury; is inverted in Hg supported by the atmosphere at sea level varies; it is not always 760 mm: The mete- dish of mercury: Mercury flows out of the orologist who says ~low" is approaching means that the atmospheric pressure is going tube until the pressure of the column of to decrease. This condition often occurs in conjunction with storm mercury (shown by the black arTow) "stand- Atmospheric pressure also varies with altitude. For example when Torricelli $ ex- ing on the surface of the mercury in the periment is done in Breckenridge. Colorado (elevation 9600 feet) . the atmosphere sup- dish is equal to the pressure of the air (shown by the purple arrows) on the rest of ports column of mercury only about 520 mm high because the air is " thinner:' That is. the surface of the mercury in the dish_ there is less air pushing down on the earth's surface at Breckenridge than at sea level:
